question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user who controls a DNS record for the mirror REST hostname cause newClient() to build a hiero.Client via hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) pointing entirely to attacker-owned gRPC endpoints, so that all subsequent transactions are submitted to a rogue node that fabricates receipts and never forwards them to the real Hashgraph, effectively erasing those transactions from the ledger history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cfg.network == 'other' branch in newClient() by serving a crafted /api/v1/network/nodes JSON response that returns only attacker-controlled ServiceEndpoints, causing hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) to route all CryptoTransfer transactions to a node that silently drops them, producing a gap in Hashgraph transaction history that cannot be detected by the pinger?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user perform an HTTP man-in-the-middle attack against the unencrypted HTTP call inside buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() (cfg.mirrorRest uses plain http://) to inject a nodesEnvelope payload containing only rogue node entries, causing the returned netmap to map all AccountIDs to attacker-controlled gRPC addresses and allowing the attacker to forge transaction receipts that misrepresent Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user who can respond to the HTTP GET in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() inject a nodeEntry with a DomainName pointing to an attacker server, causing hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) to submit signed CryptoTransfer transactions to that server, where the attacker can replay or withhold them to manipulate the observable Hashgraph transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS certificate pinning in the http.Client{Timeout: cfg.mirrorNodeClientTimeout} used by fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to present a fraudulent TLS certificate and serve a poisoned nodesEnvelope, redirecting the Hiero client's node map to attacker infrastructure and causing all pinger transactions to be submitted off-ledger?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the default http.Client redirect-following behavior in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() by injecting an HTTP 301/302 redirect from the mirror REST endpoint to an attacker-controlled server, causing the node map to be populated with rogue gRPC endpoints and all subsequent Hashgraph transactions to be silently discarded?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a nodesEnvelope JSON response in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() where every nodeEntry has a valid NodeAccountID but ServiceEndpoints pointing to 127.0.0.1 on a port the attacker controls locally, causing hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) to route transactions to localhost and preventing any real Hashgraph history from being written?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user set the HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST environment variable (e.g., via a misconfigured container environment) to an attacker-controlled URL before newClient() is called, causing buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to fetch a poisoned node list and hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) to build a client that submits all transactions to rogue nodes, tampering with the Hashgraph history observable by the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newClient() performs no cryptographic verification of the node list returned by buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes(), allowing a network-adjacent attacker to substitute any set of gRPC endpoints and cause the pinger to submit transactions that are never recorded on the real Hashgraph, creating a false impression of liveness?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user cause buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to return a netmap containing a mix of legitimate and rogue node entries by injecting extra nodeEntry objects into the nodesEnvelope, causing the SDK to occasionally route transactions to attacker nodes and selectively suppress specific transaction IDs from Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&payload) call in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() by serving an oversized or deeply nested JSON body that causes excessive memory allocation, crashing the pinger process and preventing any further transactions from being submitted to the Hashgraph, creating a history gap?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a nodesEnvelope with a NodeAccountID that parses successfully via hiero.AccountIDFromString() but maps to a different account than the real consensus node, causing the SDK to associate rogue gRPC endpoints with legitimate node account IDs and silently misroute transactions away from the real Hashgraph?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cfg.network == 'other' code path in newClient() combined with a DNS rebinding attack against cfg.mirrorRest to first pass the initial HTTP fetch from a legitimate IP and then rebind to an attacker IP for subsequent requests, causing the node map to be poisoned after initial validation and all transactions to be routed to rogue nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the retry loop in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() (up to cfg.mirrorNodeClientMaxRetries+1 attempts) by making the first N-1 attempts return HTTP 500 and the final attempt return a poisoned nodesEnvelope, ensuring the attacker's node list is accepted while exhausting legitimate retry budget?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() only checks resp.StatusCode for 2xx and does not validate Content-Type, allowing the attacker to serve a JSON payload disguised as a legitimate mirror node response that injects rogue ServiceEndpoints into the Hiero client's network map?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a serviceEndpoint with an IPAddressV4 value containing a newline or null byte that survives strings.TrimSpace() and is passed to net.JoinHostPort(), causing the resulting address string to be malformed in a way that the SDK silently ignores, reducing the effective node set and concentrating all transactions on fewer nodes to make history manipulation easier?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence the HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY environment variable supply the hardcoded default key (302e020100300506032b65700422042091132178e72057a1d7528025956fe39b0b847f200ab59b2fdd367017f3087137) to newClient() via hiero.PrivateKeyFromString(), allowing them to sign transactions as the default operator and submit crafted transfers that pollute Hashgraph history with attacker-chosen data?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the publicly known default operatorKey value hardcoded in config.go (used when HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY is unset) to reconstruct the operator's private key, then independently sign and submit transactions to the Hashgraph that appear to originate from the pinger's operator account, injecting false history entries?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.PrivateKeyFromString(cfg.operatorKey) in newClient() accepts keys from environment variables without any entropy or format validation beyond basic parsing, allowing an attacker who can set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY to a weak or known key to take over the operator identity and forge Hashgraph transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cfg.network switch in newClient() by setting HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK to 'other' and HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to an attacker-controlled URL, bypassing the hiero.ClientForName() path (which uses hardcoded trusted endpoints) and instead building a fully attacker-controlled network map via hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap)?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the URL construction logic in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() — where the base URL is checked for a /api/v1 suffix — by supplying a cfg.mirrorRest value ending in /api/v1 to skip the suffix append, causing the request to go to a different path on an attacker server that returns a poisoned node list?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of response body size limits in json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&payload) within fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to serve a multi-gigabyte JSON response that causes the pinger to OOM-crash, preventing legitimate Hashgraph transactions from being submitted and creating an exploitable history gap?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user serve a nodesEnvelope where the Nodes array is empty, causing fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to return the error 'no usable service_endpoints found', which propagates through buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() and causes newClient() to fail, preventing the pinger from ever submitting transactions and creating a permanent gap in Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the nodesEnvelope.Links.Next field being parsed but never followed in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork(), causing the pinger to only see a partial (attacker-curated) first page of nodes and build a network map that excludes legitimate consensus nodes, concentrating all transactions on a subset of nodes that the attacker can more easily monitor or manipulate?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newClient() calls client.SetOperator(opID, opKey) after building the network map, meaning that if the network map is poisoned, the operator's private key is transmitted (as part of signed transactions) to rogue nodes, allowing the attacker to extract the key and subsequently forge arbitrary Hashgraph history entries signed by the legitimate operator?
